macro_segments.title: The Exemption of Kohanim and Levi'im from Pidyon Peter Chamor: Abaye's Animal-Substitution Model

micro_segments.title: Introduction to the Mishna: Kohanim and Levi'im Exempt from Redemption of the Firstborn Donkey
The Kal Vachomer Argument: If Levi'im Exempted Israel's Firstborn, They Certainly Exempt Their Own
Abaye's Interpretation: The Animals of the Levi'im, Not the Levi'im Themselves, Provide the Exemption
Raba's Objection to Abaye: Language of the Mishna Does Not Support Abaye's Animal-Based Exemption
Raba's Second Objection: If Animals Exempt Based on the Midbar Model, Why Not for Firstborn Kosher Animals?

macro_segments.title: Raba's Alternative: Exemption Based on Personal Status of Kohen/Levi, Linked to Pidyon Bechor of Humans

micro_segments.title: Raba's Reading of the Mishna: The Sanctity of Levi'im Themselves Exempts, Not Their Animals
The Conceptual Basis: How a Levi's Status Substituted for a Firstborn Yisrael in the Midbar
Why Levi'im Are Exempt from Pidyon Bechor of Humans: Rashi's Explanation of the Firstborn Levi'im
The Gezerah Shavah: Linking the Redemption of Humans to the Redemption of Donkeys
Why Kosher Animals Remain Obligatory: The Difference Between Redemption and Korban
The Closure of the Reasoning: Levi'im Exempt from Both Peter Chamor and Pidyon Bechor, But Obligated for Bechor Behemah Tehorah

macro_segments.title: Rav Pappa's Challenges to the Midbar-Based Exemptions: Case-by-Case Problems

micro_segments.title: First Challenge: If Exemption Requires an Animal Substitute, What If a Levi Has Only Donkeys and No Sheep?
Second Challenge: Age Requirement—A Baby Levi Less Than One Month Old Was Not Counted in the Midbar
Third Challenge: Mother's Status—A Levi Woman Married to a Non-Levi Had a Son Not Counted in the Midbar

macro_segments.title: Resolution: Shifting from Narrative Precedent to Halachic Principles

micro_segments.title: The Gemara's Answer on Mother's Status: The Womb-Breaching Requirement Shows Women Matter in the Halacha
Expanding the Narrative: Using the Midbar Story as a Paradigm, Then Deriving Broader Halachic Rules
Reconciling Narrative and Law: Why We Cannot Limit the Exemption to Only What Happened in the Midbar

macro_segments.title: The Universal Exemption of All Levi'im: Using Scriptural Language to Generalize Beyond Individual Cases

micro_segments.title: Aharon's Case: He Was Not Counted, Yet Must Still Be Exempt from Peter Chamor
The Verse 'HaLevi'im': All Levi'im Are Connected to One Another in Their Exemption
Kohanim as Levi'im: Twenty-Four Places in Scripture Where Kohanim Are Called Levi'im
Perpetuity of the Status: 'Vehayou Li HaLevi'im'—The Exemption Extends to Future Generations

macro_segments.title: The Relationship Between Narrative and Halacha: How Law and History Inform Each Other

micro_segments.title: Can We Read Halacha Back into the Narrative? The Case of Sheep Substituting for Donkeys in the Midbar
The Parallel Between Money and Sheep: Money for Humans, Sheep for Donkeys, Across Both Narrative and Law
Money's Multiple Uses in Redemption: Why Money Is More Likely to Appear in Both Narrative and Halacha
The Gezerah Shavah of 'Padoh Tifdeh': Connecting Redemption of Humans and Donkeys in the Verse
One Sheep for Many Donkeys: The Symbolic Nature of the Midbar Exchange Was One-to-Many, Not One-to-One
Why the Torah Did Not Count Animal Redemptions: The Symbolic One-to-Many Model Explains the Absence of Detailed Accounting
Applying the Midbar Model to Halacha: Using One Animal to Redeem Multiple Donkeys in Certain Cases

topical_tags.term: Kohanim u'Levi'im peturin (Kohanim and Levi'im exempt)
Behemot Levi'im tachat behemotam (animals of Levi'im in exchange for animals of Israel)
Abaye (Amora who argues for animal-substitution model)
Raba (Amora who argues for personal status exemption)
Rav Pappa (Amora who challenges the Midbar-based reasoning)
Peter rechem (womb-breacher, firstborn opening the womb)
Aharon (case of someone not counted but still exempt)
HaLevi'im (the Levi'im—plural definiteness as indicator of universality)
Vehayou li HaLevi'im (They shall be for Me—perpetuity of Levi status)
Narrative vs. Halacha (relationship between story and law)
Rav Chisda (Amora explaining how to learn halacha from narrative)
Rav Chanina (Amora on one-to-many redemption in Midbar)
Padoh tifdeh (you shall surely redeem—verse linking humans and donkeys)
